title: Resolve JAL one stage earlier
motivation:
CoreMark contains frequent unconditional calls and loop-control jumps. The current
pipeline treats JAL like every other control transfer and redirects from EX, so each
taken JAL pays the same two wrong-path bubbles as a conditional branch even though
its target is fully known from the instruction bits in decode.

Add a decode/IF fast path for legal JAL instructions: compute the J-immediate target
from the IF/ID instruction and redirect the PC before the instruction reaches EX,
while preserving the normal EX-stage JAL result and RVFI pc_wdata bookkeeping for
retirement. JALR and conditional branches remain EX-resolved because they require
register operands or comparison results. This should reduce CoreMark cycles by
removing one redirect bubble from unconditional direct jumps with minimal timing cost.

title: Add decoupled sequential fetch queue
motivation: CoreMark is evaluated with roughly 22% randomized instruction and data bus backpressure. The current front end is fully coupled to the pipeline: any imem miss-ready cycle injects a bubble, and dmem stalls prevent useful sequential fetching. A small in-order fetch queue can bank instructions during cycles when the back end is blocked and then drain through short imem-ready gaps, improving iterations per cycle without changing the single-retire RVFI contract.

title: Static backward branch predictor
motivation:
The current five-stage core fetches sequentially until EX resolves every taken
branch or jump, so tight CoreMark loops pay a two-stage redirect bubble on
each taken back-edge. CoreMark has many small counted loops where a simple
static backward-taken, forward-not-taken predictor should remove most loop
back-edge redirects while keeping LUT and Fmax cost low.

title: Prune false load-use stalls
motivation:
The current load-use hazard check compares a load destination against both
IF/ID register fields for every following instruction. That is conservative:
I-type, load, JALR, U-type, JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK do not consume the rs2
field in EX, and store rs2 is store data that can be supplied later in MEM.
CoreMark has many immediate operations and memory copies, so these false or
avoidable bubbles directly reduce iterations per cycle.

bypass from the current WB value to the store write-data path. This preserves
precise RVFI retirement and memory masks while removing bubbles that do not

title: Add one-entry store buffer
motivation:
CoreMark is evaluated with randomized dmem backpressure, and the current pipeline
freezes all stages whenever a store in MEM sees io_dmemReady=0. Stores do not
produce a register result, so blocking fetch, decode, and execute behind every
delayed store wastes cycles that could otherwise retire independent ALU and
control-flow work. The prior structural fetch FIFO did not help enough because
it buffered only fall-through front-end work; this targets the back-end stall
source directly.

queues or buffers with broad control cost; this targets a specific pipeline
boundary that directly removes retirement bubbles.

title: Share RV32M ALU arithmetic resources
motivation:
The successful recent change already removed many load-use bubbles, while
prior front-end and predictor additions regressed from extra control and fetch
path cost. The remaining low-risk fitness opportunity is Fmax/LUT pressure in
the EX-stage ALU: the current combinational ALU unconditionally forms three
64-bit products and leaves separate divide/remainder expressions in the result
mux even though most CoreMark instructions use the simple RV32I fast path and
only occasional MUL-class operations.

is resource-shared. Use a single sign-controlled extended multiplier that

mux, improving median FPGA Fmax with essentially unchanged CoreMark cycle

behavior, and the existing cocotb ALU vectors plus cosim should guard exact

title: Move DIV/REM off EX critical path
motivation:
The current EX-stage ALU contains fully combinational RV32M division and
remainder operators. Even when CoreMark rarely executes DIV/REM inside the
timed region, synthesis must still route those large operators through the
ALU result mux, which is a plausible Fmax and LUT bottleneck on the Tang Nano
20K. Prior structural wins came from targeted pipeline-boundary changes, not
broad queues or buffering, so the best next structural angle is to remove a
rare long-latency operator from the common one-cycle EX path.

title: Refine static branch direction
motivation: The current predictor's biggest proven win is its stateless JAL plus backward-branch rule, while the recent RAS and tagged BHT attempts regressed from extra IF-stage state and muxing. title: Trim register file reset cost
motivation: The current best design already gained IPC from static direct-control prediction and MEM-to-EX load forwarding, while recent attempts that add predictor state or reshape RV32M arithmetic hurt timing. That reset fanout and x0 storage can cost LUT/FF resources and routing on the Tang Nano 20K without improving CoreMark's timed behavior.

title: Scrubbed LUTRAM register file
motivation:
The current register file is a 32x32 flip-flop array with a synchronous reset
loop over every architectural register. That costs roughly a thousand FFs plus
reset mux/fanout in a design that is already near the LUT budget, while reset
initialization happens long before the CoreMark timing markers and does not
need to be single-cycle.

data contract is preserved. Removing the wide resettable FF array should lower
FF/LUT pressure and reset routing enough to improve median Tang Nano 20K Fmax

title: One-entry JALR target predictor
motivation:
The current fetch predictor already captures the large CoreMark win from direct JAL
targets and backward conditional branches. Prior predictor attempts that added a
BHT, RAS, or richer branch heuristic regressed because they put too much state and
decode/mux logic on the IF path. The remaining predictable control-flow bubbles are
likely JALR returns and other repeated indirect jumps, but they need a much cheaper
mechanism than a stack or tagged direction table.

motivation:
The current best core gained IPC by forwarding extended MEM-stage load data
directly into EX, but that makes the MEM load extraction logic part of a hot
bypass path. The existing implementation uses variable shifts and shifted
byte masks for every access width, which can synthesize into a wider mux cone
than the four byte lanes actually require.

title: Learn one hot loop exit
motivation:
The current best predictor is the stateless JAL plus backward-branch rule, which
removes most hot loop back-edge bubbles but still misses the final fall-through
exit of fixed-count backward loops. Prior predictor attempts regressed because
they added general IF-stage tables, tags, RAS state, or indirect-target muxing.
CoreMark has stable counted loops, so a very narrow loop-exit predictor may
recover a recurring miss class without paying the cost of a BHT or BTB.

not-taken exit. When the same branch is fetched on a later run and the local
iteration counter reaches the learned exit count, override the static

title: Trim late pipeline metadata
motivation:
The current best core already gained IPC from static direct-control prediction and MEM-to-EX load forwarding, while recent micro-optimizations that touched IF prediction, the register file, RV32M sharing, or MEM lane muxing regressed. title: Word-only MEM load bypass
motivation:
The current best core gained IPC from MEM-to-EX load forwarding, but the

timing-sensitive and should be shortened rather than expanded. CoreMark's hot
pointer and array chains are expected to be dominated by aligned LW uses, while
dependent LB/LH/LBU/LHU pairs should be rarer.

title: Predicted path fetch skid buffer
motivation:
CoreMark is still evaluated with randomized instruction and data bus stalls, and the current
front end remains tightly coupled to the back end: a dmem stall freezes fetch entirely, while
an isolated imem-not-ready cycle becomes a decode bubble unless the instruction is available
immediately. The earlier structural fetch FIFO regressed because it buffered only sequential
pc+4 fetches before the static direct-control predictor existed, so it could not prefetch useful
loop/back-edge work through back-end stalls. The current best core now has a proven low-cost
JAL and backward-branch predictor, which makes a small predicted-path fetch queue more likely
to hold correct useful instructions.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure IF into a two-entry predicted-path skid buffer with separate fetch and decode sides.
The fetch side advances its private fetch PC along the existing static predicted next PC whenever
io_imemReady is high and the queue has space, including cycles where dmem backpressure is holding
ID/EX and EX/MEM. The decode side consumes the oldest queued pc/instruction/pred_next_pc only
when ID is allowed to advance; when the queue is empty, decode simply sees an invalid bubble.
Any EX redirect flushes the queue and restarts the fetch PC at the resolved target, preserving
the existing RVFI pc_rdata/pc_wdata ordering and the NRET=1 single-retire contract.

This targets the specific failure mode called out by the prior sequential FIFO lesson: it keeps
front-end decoupling, but makes it redirect-aware and predictor-aware without adding a BHT, RAS,
store buffer, or new architectural retirement channel. It should recover cycles lost to short
imem gaps after dmem holds while keeping the successful static predictor policy unchanged.

title: Encode static prediction as direction
motivation:
The current best predictor is the simple stateless rule: predict direct JAL
taken and backward conditional branches taken. Prior predictor attempts that
added BHTs, RAS/JALR state, loop counters, or extra IF target muxing all
regressed, so the next predictor hypothesis should preserve that proven policy
and reduce its implementation cost instead of adding new prediction behavior.

Today IF carries a full 32-bit pred_next_pc through IF/ID and ID/EX only so EX
can compare it with the resolved next PC. For this predictor, correctness only
depends on whether fetch predicted the direct control transfer taken or
fall-through: EX can recompute the same direct JAL/branch target from pc and imm
when needed, and JALR is still predicted fall-through.

title: Register fetch decode boundary
motivation:
The current front end has no real IF/ID register: imem_data flows through fetch prediction packaging, decoder, immediate generation, register-file address selection, and into the ID/EX register in one cycle. Prior structural queues and skid buffers regressed because their control was too broad, but the remaining Fmax pressure may still be this long fetch-to-decode boundary rather than CoreMark IPC.

A minimal registered fetch/decode boundary is a narrower structural change than a FIFO: it should cut a large timing cone and preserve the proven static direct-control predictor and MEM-to-EX load forwarding behavior.

title: Skid one ALU op behind dmem stalls
motivation: The current pipeline freezes every stage whenever a load or store in MEM waits for io_dmemReady. Prior structural attempts that added front-end queues or a store buffer regressed because they added broad control and memory-ordering muxes, but randomized dmem backpressure is still a direct IPC limiter. A narrower structural change can use the first blocked cycle behind a memory operation without changing prediction, memory ordering, or the NRET=1 RVFI contract.

hypothesis.motivation: The current pipeline freezes every stage whenever a load or store in MEM waits for io_dmemReady. Prior structural attempts that added front-end queues or a store buffer regressed because they added broad control and memory-ordering muxes, but randomized dmem backpressure is still a direct IPC limiter. A narrower structural change can use the first blocked cycle behind a memory operation without changing prediction, memory ordering, or the NRET=1 RVFI contract.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry execute skid register for safe younger instructions while EX/MEM is holding an older stalled memory operation. When dmem_stall is asserted and ID/EX holds a valid non-memory, non-branch, non-jump instruction that does not depend on the stalled load's rd, EX computes it once and captures the full ex_mem_t result into the skid entry. The front end and ID/EX then remain held until the older memory operation completes; after the memory instruction retires, the skid entry drains into the normal EX/MEM to MEM/WB path before any newly decoded instruction advances.

This is intentionally not a general store buffer, fetch FIFO, or out-of-order engine. It only hides one cycle of memory backpressure for independent straight-line ALU/LUI/AUIPC/MUL-class work, preserves strict in-order RVFI retirement by draining after the older memory op, and leaves loads, stores, branches, jumps, and load-dependent consumers on the existing conservative path. CoreMark has many pointer and loop instructions interleaved with memory traffic, so recovering even one cycle from a fraction of randomized dmem stalls should improve iterations/cycle with less hot-path cost than prior structural buffering attempts.

Wire the skid valid/payload between EX, hazard control, and MEM so the older memory operation retires first and the skidded instruction drains through channel 0 afterward.

title: Gate predictor repair compare
motivation:
The current best predictor is the stateless JAL-taken plus backward-branch-taken
rule, and the strongest recent predictor win came from making that same policy
cheaper rather than smarter. Prior attempts that added BHT, RAS, JALR memo,
loop-exit state, richer static direction, fetch buffering, adder sharing, or
JAL target gating all regressed from added front-end or control-path cost.

One remaining always-active cost of the predictor is in EX: every instruction,
including ordinary ALU and memory operations, drives the resolved-next-PC versus
pred_next_pc mismatch comparator even though only branches and jumps can ever
require predictor repair.

can bypass the 32-bit next-PC mismatch check and simply retire with pc+4 as
today. Branches and JAL/JALR continue to compute resolved_next_pc exactly as
they do now, including misaligned-target trap handling, and still compare
against pred_next_pc to repair bad predictions.

predictor pattern of adding IF-stage state or new target paths. The change is
intentionally narrower than the failed EX target-arithmetic gating attempt: it
does not gate or restructure branch target, jump target, or comparator inputs,
only the final predictor-repair decision for instructions that cannot redirect.

title: Factor forwarding matches
motivation:
The current best bench core already gained IPC from MEM-to-EX load forwarding, but that makes the forwarding selector part of a hot EX operand path. Recent attempts that added state, new stalls, or broad datapath gating regressed, so the next micro_opt should be a very small cleanup that preserves the proven pipeline behavior.

The existing forward_unit repeats the same EX/MEM and MEM/WB register-destination comparisons inside separate ALU-result, load-result, and writeback arms for each source operand. Even if synthesis can sometimes common-subexpression these terms, making the producer matches explicit should reduce comparator duplication, fanout, and select-control depth with no cycle-count or architectural risk.

title: Register final writeback value
motivation:
The current MEM/WB stage carries both alu_result and read_data, then selects the
architectural writeback value through a combinational WB mux. That mux output
fans out to the register file write port, same-cycle regfile read bypass,
MEM/WB-to-EX forwarding, and RVFI rd_wdata. Prior structural attempts that
added queues, buffers, or sidecars regressed because they added broad control;
this targets a narrower pipeline-boundary fanout point without changing IPC.

title: Drop JAL fetch prediction
motivation:
The strongest predictor lesson is that added state or richer control-flow
heuristics have repeatedly cost more FPGA timing than they saved in CoreMark
bubbles. The current IF predictor still forms a JAL immediate and JAL target
for every fetched instruction, and the recent attempt to gate that path
regressed from extra mux/control cost. If direct JALs are uncommon inside the
bracketed CoreMark hot loops after -O3 inlining, removing this predictor path
outright may recover Fmax while keeping the proven backward-branch prediction.

always-active J-immediate shuffle, JAL target adder, and JAL arm of the IF

apply to every fetch cycle.

title: Predecode forwarding selects
motivation: The most recent improvement came from factoring duplicated forwarding matches, which strongly suggests the EX operand-select path is still timing-sensitive. Prior structural attempts with FIFOs, store buffers, divider sidecars, and dmem skid slots regressed because they added broad control to common paths. A narrower structural retiming can target the same hot forwarding cone without changing CoreMark IPC or memory ordering.

EX keeps only the small operand muxes and the existing ready-qualified MEM-load handling, with a fallback or hold behavior that preserves correctness through dmem stalls and redirects.

This should shorten the common EX path from comparator-plus-priority-plus-mux to mostly muxing, building on the proven forwarding-match optimization while avoiding new queues, predictor state, or out-of-order retirement risk. title: Prioritize predictor fallthrough path
motivation:
The only durable predictor win in the bench core is the stateless direct-control policy: predict JAL taken and backward conditional branches taken. Follow-on attempts that added BHT, RAS, JALR memo, loop-exit state, richer static direction, target-adder sharing, JAL gating, or predictor repair gating mostly regressed because extra IF or EX control cost outweighed the remaining saved bubbles.

The current IF predictor still presents a three-way predicted-next-PC mux over pc+4, backward-branch target, and JAL target on every fetch. CoreMark's hot path is dominated by ordinary fall-through instructions plus loop branches, so making fall-through the explicit fast/default predictor path may reduce fetch timing pressure without changing the successful prediction behavior.

title: One-hot forwarding selects
motivation:
The recent successful micro-opt showed that the EX operand forwarding selector is still a timing-sensitive path after MEM-to-EX load forwarding. The current forward unit already factors producer matches, but it still encodes each source choice into a two-bit select that EX immediately decodes back into a four-way operand mux.

Prior attempts to move forwarding decisions earlier regressed because dmem stalls and producer validity made stale hints expensive to repair. A smaller dynamic encoding cleanup can keep the same late, correct match logic while reducing select decode depth on the hot operand path.

title: Register memory request metadata
motivation:
The current best core already gets its IPC wins from static fetch prediction and
MEM-to-EX load forwarding, while broad structural changes such as fetch queues,
store buffers, IF/ID registration, divider sidecars, EX skid slots, and forwarding
predecode have repeatedly lost timing or control simplicity. A more promising
structural target is the MEM boundary itself: mem_stage still derives byte masks,
replicated store data, aligned RVFI memory addresses, and misalignment traps
combinationally from EX/MEM on the same cycle it drives dmem outputs and load
forwarding.

title: Trim predicted PC alignment bits
motivation: Prior predictor work shows the current stateless JAL-taken plus backward-branch-taken policy is the only durable control-flow win. Attempts to add BHT, RAS, JALR memo, loop-exit learning, richer static direction, target sharing, JAL gating, or fall-through rewrites mostly lost Fmax. The safest remaining predictor angle is therefore to keep the exact policy and reduce only the metadata/control cost around predictor repair.

hypothesis.motivation: Prior predictor work shows the current stateless JAL-taken plus backward-branch-taken policy is the only durable control-flow win. Attempts to add BHT, RAS, JALR memo, loop-exit learning, richer static direction, target sharing, JAL gating, or fall-through rewrites mostly lost Fmax. The safest remaining predictor angle is therefore to keep the exact policy and reduce only the metadata/control cost around predictor repair.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Carry only pred_next_pc[31:2] through IF/ID and ID/EX, because this RV32IM core has no compressed extension and all architecturally valid fetch PCs are word-aligned. IF still computes the same predicted next PC for its local PC update, while EX reconstructs or compares the aligned high bits when checking resolved_next_pc against the prediction. Misaligned branch or jump targets already trap and use pc+4 as the resolved next PC, so the low two predicted bits are redundant for legal retirement and RVFI pc_wdata remains driven from the resolved next PC.

This preserves the proven prediction behavior and all redirect timing semantics, unlike the failed direction-bit rewrite that forced EX to recompute predictor targets. The expected benefit is small but low-risk: two fewer carried predictor bits in early pipeline payloads and a slightly narrower predictor-repair compare, with no new tables, tags, target muxes, or update state.

title: Alias load writeback select
motivation:
The best recent bench improvements came from very localized timing cleanups: isolating the RV32M ALU mux, factoring forwarding matches, and registering memory request metadata. Broader rewrites of forwarding, late metadata, the register file, and fetch/control state have usually regressed from extra muxing or routing perturbation.

A small remaining redundant control bit is `mem_to_reg`. In this core it is asserted exactly for loads, while `mem_read` is already carried through the same pipeline control bundle for memory behavior and load forwarding. Keeping both bits through decode, ID/EX, EX/MEM, and MEM/WB adds decode assignments, packed control width, and WB mux fanout without representing an independent architectural choice.

hypothesis.motivation:
The best recent bench improvements came from very localized timing cleanups: isolating the RV32M ALU mux, factoring forwarding matches, and registering memory request metadata. Broader rewrites of forwarding, late metadata, the register file, and fetch/control state have usually regressed from extra muxing or routing perturbation.

A small remaining redundant control bit is `mem_to_reg`. In this core it is asserted exactly for loads, while `mem_read` is already carried through the same pipeline control bundle for memory behavior and load forwarding. Keeping both bits through decode, ID/EX, EX/MEM, and MEM/WB adds decode assignments, packed control width, and WB mux fanout without representing an independent architectural choice.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Remove the independent `mem_to_reg` control signal and use `mem_read` as the load-result writeback selector. Loads still write `read_data`, all other register-writing instructions still write `alu_result`, and misaligned loads remain safe because their trap path clears `reg_write`; the selected WB data is ignored when the instruction does not retire or does not write a register.

This should slightly reduce late control routing and the writeback/forwarding select cone without changing cycle count, memory ordering, RVFI metadata, trap behavior, or the NRET=1 top-level port set. It deliberately avoids the prior failed broad metadata split: only one semantically redundant bit is removed, and existing stage boundaries and datapaths remain intact.

Stop wiring and storing the decoder mem_to_reg signal in ctrl_decoded while preserving all existing decode, immediate, source, and valid behavior.
Select writeback data with in.ctrl.mem_read instead of in.ctrl.mem_to_reg, keeping w_en, x0 handling, forwarding, and RVFI rd_wdata behavior unchanged.

title: Retimed WB-to-ID register bypass
motivation:
The current core keeps write-first bypass muxes inside the integer register file.
That makes the WB-stage write data and destination comparators part of both
combinational read ports, even though the only architectural need is that the
value captured into ID/EX reflects same-cycle writeback. Prior register-file
storage rewrites regressed because they changed the physical array and reset
behavior; this is a narrower structural retiming of the bypass boundary.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current core keeps write-first bypass muxes inside the integer register file.
That makes the WB-stage write data and destination comparators part of both
combinational read ports, even though the only architectural need is that the
value captured into ID/EX reflects same-cycle writeback. Prior register-file
storage rewrites regressed because they changed the physical array and reset
behavior; this is a narrower structural retiming of the bypass boundary.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Make reg_file a simpler x0-guarded asynchronous read plus synchronous write
array, and move the same-cycle WB bypass into id_stage at the point where
rs1_val and rs2_val are captured into the ID/EX register. Core wires the WB
enable, address, and final writeback data into ID so decode can select the
just-written value only for the two source operands entering the pipeline.

This preserves the existing architectural behavior, EX forwarding priority,
RVFI source-value reporting, and NRET=1 retirement contract, but removes the
high-fanout writeback mux/data path from the regfile read module and may give
synthesis a cleaner ID-stage boundary. It should improve Fmax slightly with no
CoreMark cycle-count cost and much less risk than another queue, store buffer,
early redirect, or divider sidecar.

changes.description: Remove internal same-cycle write-first read bypass muxes while keeping x0 hardwired, synchronous writes, and reset behavior unchanged.
Add explicit WB bypass inputs and select bypassed rs1_val/rs2_val when the instruction source matches the same-cycle writeback destination.
Wire wb_w_en, wb_w_addr, and wb_w_data into id_stage so the core-level behavior and RVFI source metadata remain unchanged.

lesson: - 2026-05-12 hyp-20260512-002-r15s1 (regression, -32.24%): Retiming same-cycle WB bypass out of the reg file and into ID/EX capture can worsen timing by putting WB rd/data compares and 32-bit source muxes on the decode register boundary, so write-first RF behavior may be the cleaner placement.

title: Selective JAL fetch prediction
motivation:
The durable predictor win for this core is still the stateless direct-control
policy: predict backward conditional branches taken and avoid adding IF-stage
tables, tags, RAS state, loop counters, or indirect-target compares. Prior
attempts to make the predictor smarter or syntactically rearrange the same
muxes mostly regressed from fetch-path cost. The remaining useful predictor
angle is therefore a policy simplification that removes part of the expensive
JAL target cone while preserving the loop/back-edge behavior most likely to
matter inside CoreMark.

hypothesis.motivation:
The durable predictor win for this core is still the stateless direct-control
policy: predict backward conditional branches taken and avoid adding IF-stage
tables, tags, RAS state, loop counters, or indirect-target compares. Prior
attempts to make the predictor smarter or syntactically rearrange the same
muxes mostly regressed from fetch-path cost. The remaining useful predictor

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the existing backward-branch predictor exactly as-is, but make direct
JAL prediction selective: predict only linkless JAL x0 jumps and backward JAL
targets in IF, and let forward call-style JAL instructions fall through to the
existing EX redirect repair path. This preserves prediction for unconditional
loop jumps and other local backward control flow, while avoiding the J-immediate
shuffle and JAL target adder on the common path for forward calls that are less
likely to dominate the bracketed CoreMark loops after inlining.

The change is intentionally narrower than adding a BTB/RAS/BHT and less drastic
than dropping all JAL prediction. Bad selective predictions remain ordinary
EX-stage redirects because resolved_next_pc is still compared with pred_next_pc,
so RVFI pc_wdata, trap handling, and the NRET=1 retirement contract are unchanged.

changes.description: Change the static fetch predictor so direct JAL targets are predicted only for linkless or backward JALs, while preserving the current backward-branch prediction and EX redirect repair semantics.

lesson: - 2026-05-12 hyp-20260512-003-r15s2 (regression, -19.33%): Selective JAL prediction by rd=x0/backward sign is worse than the coarse always-JAL rule because the added IF rd/sign gating and lost forward-call prediction outweigh any J-immediate target-cone pruning.
